tip: Dogs need at least 30 minutes of walking per day. Regular walks support cardiovascular health, healthy weight, and reduce anxiety-driven behaviours like chewing or barking.
Feed pets at consistent times each day. Irregular meal schedules can disrupt digestion and cause stress. Always provide fresh water alongside every meal.
Indoor cats need daily play sessions of at least 15 minutes to prevent boredom and obesity. Wand toys, puzzle feeders, and window perches are excellent sources of stimulation.
Brush your pet's coat at least once a week to reduce shedding, prevent matting, and spot skin issues early. Long-haired breeds may need daily brushing.
Annual vet check-ups catch illnesses before they become serious. Keep vaccinations, flea, and tick treatments up to date even if your pet appears healthy.
Mental stimulation is as important as physical exercise for dogs. Five minutes of training new tricks or scent games each day can significantly reduce restless behaviour.
Trim your cat's nails every two to three weeks to prevent overgrowth and accidental scratching. Use dedicated pet nail clippers and avoid cutting into the pink quick.
Puppies under six months benefit from short, frequent walks of 5 minutes per month of age. Early socialisation during walks builds confidence and reduces fear-based aggression.
Senior cats (7+ years) have changing nutritional needs. Switch to a senior formula diet and watch for weight loss, increased thirst, or changes in litter-box habits.
Pets should drink roughly 30–50 ml of water per kg of body weight daily. A water fountain can encourage cats and dogs that prefer running water to stay well hydrated.
Most dogs only need a bath every four to six weeks. Over-bathing strips natural oils from the coat. Use a mild, pet-safe shampoo and dry thoroughly to prevent skin irritation.
Rotate your pet's toys every few days to maintain novelty. Pets quickly lose interest in the same toy, but brief periods away make it feel new again, sustaining engagement.
